smartlead_campaign: OR #3 | Ecom Leads | Scroll Stopper | Personlization

magnet.report_label: Scroll-Stopper Sheet (the 3 ads we wrote them)

thread.subject: something for LPC
thread.body_text:
hey Leilani, noticed LPC Golf's founder never touched leather or a sewing machine before starting, curious how that came about.

on a separate note: I built 3 meta ad mockups from LPC's own products, plus a world-class brand playbook from your site and reviews.

 no pitch attached, it's already built.

 should I send it?

- Kyle

 P.S. you gotta show people what an amazing brand you got for real!!

thread.subject: RE: something for LPC
thread.body_text:
Leilani - the part most brands react to isn't even the ads, it's the brand playbook. it lays out your voice, the exact language your customers use in reviews, and what you can and can't say in ad copy.

it's all set to send alongside the mockups. want me to send both over?

- Kyle

I am open to a conversation.  

 Best, 
Leilani

Love it - as promised in my first email, here are your 3 ad mockups built from LPC's own products, plus your brand playbook. 

 the mockups show the angles, the playbook is the part most brands actually keep - your voice, the words your customers use, and what you can and can't say in ad copy.

 if any of them click, the quickest way to see how I'd actually run them is a 30 min call. → Calendar 

 - Kyle

thread.subject: Re: RE: something for LPC
thread.body_text:
Hi Kyle,

I've booked some time on our calendar for Monday.

I had one quick piece of feedback on the mockups: the golf bag in image 2 appears way too small in comparison to the rest of the objects, but the others look great.

Also, just as a heads-up, our site is a custom Hydrogen build. I'm a software engineer, so I can implement changes myself, but please note that it is not a standard Shopify template.

Best,
Leilani

Leilani - fair call on the bag scale. the mockups were built from public site assets only, so think of them as angle concepts - with your actual product files they get a lot sharper. good problem to have.

 Hydrogen build is no issue at all, and having a software engineer on the brand side actually speeds everything up.

 talk Monday :)

 - Kyle

copy.tldr: LPC makes premium handmade leather golf headcovers in San Francisco, they run zero ads today, and your job Monday is to sell a slow, careful start that proves one ad can win before spending real money.

deep_dive.what_they_are: A small craft brand called LPC Golf, legally Little Prince Customs, based in San Francisco. Headcovers are hand-stitched by one artisan named Karl. The website is a custom Hydrogen build, not a normal Shopify template, and Leilani their contact is a software engineer who can make site changes herself.
deep_dive.what_they_sell: Handmade, customizable leather golf headcovers. Three named collections on the site: Quilted with 20 designs, Exotic Classic with 2 designs in embossed cowhide, and Artisan with 8 designs including rare-material pieces. They also do custom embroidery of initials, logos, or designs. No prices are public, so do not name any.
deep_dive.positioning: The premium, heritage-craft end of golf accessories. Artisan made, personalized, built to last. Not mass produced. The whole pitch is craftsmanship and standing out on the course.
deep_dive.who_buys: Discerning golfers who care about craftsmanship and personal style, and will pay more for a handmade leather cover that lasts and gets noticed. This is a higher-price, lower-volume buyer, not a bargain shopper.
customer_voice: These headcovers are absolutely amazing! The craftsmanship is top-notch and I get compliments on them every time I'm on the course.
strengths: Clear, focused brand with a real story: one artisan, made in the USA, genuine craft. That is easy to build ad angles around.
They sell customization and offer a build-a-set discount at 4+ covers, so there is a natural way to lift order value.
gaps: Almost no visible social proof. Only one review is shown on the whole site. That makes paid ads harder because new buyers have little to trust.
They have never run Meta ads, so we are starting from zero. No pixel history, no proven audience, no winning creative yet.

game_plan.headline: Start with one proven angle on their signature Quilted line, prove one ad can sell, then widen and add customization as the upsell.

phases.goal: Prove that a single ad can turn a cold golfer into a buyer. We are not chasing scale, we are finding one thing that works.
campaigns: One sales campaign, cold audience, testing the 3 ads from the Scroll-Stopper Sheet against each other.
A simple retargeting campaign for people who visited the site or viewed a product but did not buy.
lead_products: Lead with the Quilted collection. It has 20 designs, the most of any collection, which usually means it is their bestseller and the safest thing to put money behind first. It is also the most photogenic and easiest to explain in a scroll: hand-sewn diamond leather that looks premium in a single image.
phases.creative: Run the 3 ads we already wrote. Lead with the one that shows the craftsmanship close up, since that is their strongest and most honest selling point and matches the review language about top-notch craftsmanship. Hold back the ad with the golf bag Leilani flagged as too small until we rebuild it with her real product files. Say that on the call so she knows we listened.
phases.budget: Small and steady. See estimates below. The goal this month is learning, not revenue.
phases.what_success_looks_like: One of the three ads clearly beats the others and produces sales at a cost the margin can carry. We also start collecting real reviews from those first buyers.

phases.goal: Put more money behind the winning ad and widen who sees it.
campaigns: Scale the winning ad from month one with a larger daily budget.
Test 2 to 3 new versions of the winner using different designs from the Quilted line.
Broaden the audience beyond the tightest golf interests to find new buyers.
lead_products: Keep Quilted as the hero. Introduce a second Quilted design as a fresh creative so we do not burn out the first one.
phases.creative: Rebuild the bag ad properly with Leilani's real product files now that scale is fixed. Add any new reviews we collected in month one straight into the ads as social proof.
phases.budget: Step budget up only on the proven winner. Do not raise spend on anything unproven.
phases.what_success_looks_like: The winning ad holds up at higher spend and the return stays healthy as the audience widens.

phases.goal: Scale what is working and add a second product to grow order value.
campaigns: Push the proven setup harder with controlled budget increases.
Add a campaign around custom embroidery and the build-a-set, save 10% offer to lift average order size.
Introduce the Exotic Classic or Artisan collection as a second hero for buyers who want something rarer.
lead_products: Custom embroidery as an upsell, since it is personal and hard to copy. Plus the Artisan or Exotic Classic line for buyers who want a statement piece and will pay the most.
phases.creative: Lean into the build-a-set discount to raise order value, and use the personalization angle, which fits their Made for You voice.
phases.budget: Highest of the three months, but only behind proven winners.
phases.what_success_looks_like: Steady daily sales from the scaled winner, plus a lift in average order value from embroidery and set bundles.

estimates.label: Starting budget
estimates.basis: enough to get a handful of clicks a day at typical costs for a premium niche product, which is the floor for learning which of the 3 ads works, without risking real money before anything is proven

estimates.label: Revenue we would aim to add
estimates.value: We cannot give an honest number yet
estimates.basis: we have no access to their prices, margin, or website conversion rate, and they have no ad history to base a return on. Once we know their average order value and margin on the call, we can put a real target on it.

assumptions: Quilted is their bestseller. This is a guess from it having 20 designs versus 2 and 8 for the others. If Karl says a different line sells best, we lead with that instead.
Their order value and margin are high enough to carry paid ads. Handmade leather usually is, but we need the numbers to confirm it.
The site converts visitors well enough to send paid traffic to. We have not seen it live and cannot see their conversion rate.
They can keep up with custom orders if sales pick up, since it is one artisan hand-stitching everything.

questions.question: Have you ever run Meta or Instagram ads before, even a boosted post?
questions.listening_for: We saw no ads running. Confirm they are truly starting from zero. If they tried before and stopped, ask what happened, because that changes everything.

questions.question: Who handles marketing today, and how much time do they have for it?
questions.listening_for: Is this Karl doing everything, or does Leilani own marketing? Small teams mean we keep the plan simple and do more of the work ourselves.

questions.question: What does a typical headcover sell for, and roughly what is left after materials and labor?
questions.listening_for: This is the number that decides if ads can work. Handmade leather should carry a healthy margin. If margin is thin because everything is hand-stitched by one person, that limits what we can spend to get a sale.

questions.question: About how many covers can Karl make in a week?
questions.listening_for: One artisan is a real limit. If ads work, can they even fulfill the orders? We must not sell demand they cannot make.

questions.question: When someone lands on your site, what share of them actually buy? Even a rough sense.
questions.listening_for: If very few buy, the problem is the site or trust, not the ads, and we fix that first. If it converts fine, we can send paid traffic sooner.

questions.question: Which collection actually sells the most, Quilted, Exotic Classic, or Artisan?
questions.listening_for: I assumed Quilted because it has the most designs. If they say otherwise, we swap the hero product in month one immediately.

questions.question: You only show one review on the site. Do you have more reviews or photos of covers on the course sitting somewhere?
questions.listening_for: Social proof is their biggest gap. If they have reviews and customer photos hidden in email or Instagram, that is gold for ads. If they have almost none, month one has to also start collecting them.

questions.question: How long does a custom order take from checkout to shipped?
questions.listening_for: Long lead times hurt ad performance because buyers hesitate. We need to know so we set the right expectation in the ads.

questions.question: Do most sales come from custom or from ready-made designs?
questions.listening_for: Custom items cannot be returned, which raises buyer hesitation. If custom is most of the business, we need ad copy that removes that fear.

questions.question: Do you get sales from people searching Google for things like custom golf headcovers or leather headcovers?
questions.listening_for: People clearly search for golf headcovers, so there is a real Google opening here, unlike a brand new category. If they get no search traffic today, that is money left on the table we can raise later, not on this call.

questions.question: Does your Hydrogen site show up on Google when you search your product type, or only your brand name?
questions.listening_for: Since Leilani is an engineer, SEO is worth a mention. If they only rank for their own name, there is search demand for the product type they are missing. Note it, do not pitch hard yet.

objections.objection: We have never run ads before, how do I know this will work?
objections.answer: That is exactly why we start small and slow. Month one is not about big results, it is about proving one of the three ads I already showed you can sell at a cost your margin can handle. We only spend more once something works. You are not betting big up front.

objections.objection: Karl hand-stitches everything. What if the ads work and we cannot keep up?
objections.answer: That is a good problem and we plan for it. We start at a low daily budget and only raise spend as fast as you can make covers. If orders get close to capacity, we slow the ads down. We will never sell demand you cannot fulfill.

objections.objection: Our site barely has reviews, will people trust ads for a brand they have never heard of?
objections.answer: You are right that this is the weak spot, and I saw it too. Part of month one is turning your first ad buyers into reviews and course photos, then feeding those straight back into the ads. Trust builds fast once real golfers are shown holding your covers.

objections.objection: Our site is a custom Hydrogen build, not normal Shopify. Will that be a problem?
objections.answer: Not at all, and having you as an engineer on the brand side actually makes it faster. We tell you exactly what to add for tracking and you can put it in yourself, no waiting on a theme or a developer.

red_flags.flag: Almost no visible social proof. Only one review on the whole site.
red_flags.why_it_matters: Cold golfers need proof before paying a premium price. With so little visible trust, the first ads will work harder. We need to collect reviews early, not assume they exist.

red_flags.flag: Everything is hand-stitched by one artisan.
red_flags.why_it_matters: Fulfillment is a hard ceiling. If ads work, they may not be able to make enough. We must scale spend to match what Karl can produce, not faster.

red_flags.flag: No prices or product feed are public and no ads have ever run.
red_flags.why_it_matters: We are guessing at their bestseller and cannot judge margin or order value yet. Do not state any revenue or return numbers on the call. Get the real numbers first.
If most sales are custom, buyers hesitate more because there is no safety net. Ad copy has to handle that fear directly or conversions suffer.

other_channel_openings: Google Search: golfers clearly search for terms like custom golf headcovers and leather headcovers, so there is genuine existing demand to capture, unlike a made-up category. Worth raising as a later phase.

SEO: Leilani is a software engineer on a custom Hydrogen site, so she can act on SEO fixes fast. If they only rank for their brand name and not the product type, there is search traffic they are missing. Mention it, do not push it on this call.
